findings.title: Triple sync file read per ticker in processOne hot path
findings.description: For every ticker whose snapshot is younger than FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_MS (the ~80% price-only fast path), processOne calls three separate synchronous probe functions in sequence: _getExistingSnapshotAge (opens + reads 500 bytes), _existingSnapshotMissingTag211lFields (full readFileSync + JSON.parse of the ~50 KB snapshot), and _existingSnapshotMissingCurrencyNormalization (another full readFileSync + JSON.parse of the same file). All three open the exact same file.
findings.mechanism: _getExistingSnapshotAge uses fs.openSync+readSync+closeSync (500 bytes). _existingSnapshotMissingTag211lFields calls fs.readFileSync + JSON.parse (full file). _existingSnapshotMissingCurrencyNormalization calls fs.readFileSync + JSON.parse (full file again). All three run synchronously on the event loop, blocking all concurrent worker slots for the duration. At 10 concurrent workers and 15k tickers with ~80% hitting the fast path, this is ~12k * 2 = 24,000 extra blocking file reads per run beyond what is necessary.
findings.reproducer: Run with PULL_CONCURRENCY=10 against a universe of 15k tickers where >80% have snapshots younger than FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS=7. All workers serialize on event loop during the two full JSON.parse probes for each fast-path ticker.
findings.impact: Adds multiple minutes to wall-clock run time due to 24k unnecessary synchronous I/O operations blocking the event loop. The two schema/currency probes each do redundant work: after the first JSON.parse the result could be passed to the second check without re-reading. Also blocks the worker pool -- every other worker's async I/O is delayed while the event loop is blocked parsing a 50 KB JSON file synchronously.
findings.suggested_fix: Merge all three probes into a single function that reads the snapshot file once. Example: function _probeExistingSnapshot(ticker) { const fp = ...; try { const buf = Buffer.alloc(500); const fd = fs.openSync(fp,'r'); fs.readSync(fd,buf,0,500,0); fs.closeSync(fd); const ageMs = ...; if (ageMs >= FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_MS) return { youngEnough: false }; const raw = fs.readFileSync(fp,'utf8'); const s = JSON.parse(raw); return { youngEnough: true, staleSchema: _checkTag211l(s), staleCurrency: _checkCurrency(s), parsed: s }; } catch { return null; } }. Pass parsed to _priceOnlyUpdate to avoid a fourth read (see F-PF-002).

findings.title: Fourth synchronous readFileSync on same snapshot file in price-only path
findings.description: _priceOnlyUpdate calls fs.readFileSync + JSON.parse on the snapshot file (line 1399) when the youngEnough && !staleSchema && !staleCurrency condition is true. After the three probes in F-PF-001 have already parsed the file twice, _priceOnlyUpdate reads and parses the entire file again to get the existing snapshot data for the price-only merge. This is a fourth open+read+parse of the same file in the same processOne call for the ~80% fast path.
findings.mechanism: _priceOnlyUpdate receives only stock and outputDir, not the already-parsed snapshot object. It reconstructs the filepath, calls fs.readFileSync, then JSON.parse. This runs synchronously on the event loop. Combined with F-PF-001, the fast path does 3-4 synchronous blocking file reads per ticker.
findings.reproducer: Any ticker where youngEnough=true, staleSchema=false, staleCurrency=false. Approximately 80% of the universe per day after the schema-stale wave has settled.
findings.impact: Additional event-loop blocking on every price-only refresh. The cumulative 3-4 sync reads for the 80% fast path dominate pipeline wall time for large universes. The parsed object from _existingSnapshotMissingTag211lFields is discarded and re-parsed by _priceOnlyUpdate.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the already-parsed snapshot object from the merged probe (see F-PF-001 fix) into _priceOnlyUpdate as an optional third parameter `existingSnapshot`. When provided, skip the readFileSync+JSON.parse. Signature: async function _priceOnlyUpdate(stock, outputDir, existingSnapshot = null). This reduces 4 file reads per ticker to 1 in steady state.

findings.title: Synchronous serial snapshot load in sector-medians-compute CLI blocks event loop
findings.description: The CLI entry point loads all snapshot files using a synchronous for-loop with fs.readFileSync + JSON.parse. For 15k snapshots at ~50 KB each, this is 750 MB of synchronous I/O on the main thread before any computation can begin. The event loop is completely blocked for the entire duration (estimated 30-90 seconds on typical CI I/O speeds).
findings.mechanism: for (const f of files) { stocks.push(JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(...))); } This is a sequential synchronous loop. Each readFileSync blocks until the OS returns the file data. No parallelism is possible. By contrast, generate-modes-report.js and snapshot-methods-history.js already use async batched loading (loadFilesAsync with Promise.all batches of 32-200 files) achieving near-linear I/O parallelism.
findings.reproducer: Run `node methods/sector-medians-compute.js ./snapshots` against a directory with 10k+ snapshot files.
findings.impact: Adds 30-90 seconds to CI pipeline wall time. The process appears hung while reading. Cannot leverage libuv thread pool for parallel disk reads. Any upstream caller that requires fast turnaround risks timeout.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sync load with the same async batched pattern used in snapshot-methods-history.js (loadFilesAsync, BATCH=200). Make the CLI entry point async: (async () => { const stocks = await loadFilesAsync(snapshotDir, files); const auto = computeMedians(stocks, ...); ... })().catch(e => { console.error(e); process.exit(1); });

findings.title: results and failures arrays accumulate all per-run data for entire 165-min run
findings.description: The pullAll function accumulates all ticker results in the `results` array and all failures in `failures`. Both arrays live for the entire run duration (up to 165 minutes on CI). The final manifest object (line 1995) passes results and failures verbatim and writes them as a single JSON.stringify call. For a universe of 15k tickers, this means 15k result entries in memory simultaneously throughout the run.
findings.mechanism: results.push({...}) at lines 1531, 1848, 1900. failures.push({...}) at line 1952. All entries live from creation until writeFileAtomic(fullPath, JSON.stringify(manifest)) at the end of the run. The incremental manifest writes every 100 tickers reduce SIGTERM data-loss risk but do not release any memory -- they scan the full results array via results.filter() on each call.
findings.reproducer: Full pull run with PULL_CONCURRENCY=10 over 15k tickers.
findings.impact: Peak heap usage includes all 15k result objects. The incremental manifest writeManifestIncremental() at lines 1253-1254 calls results.filter() over the full growing array on every 100-ticker boundary -- for 15k tickers this is 150 filter calls, each scanning an average of 7.5k entries = 1.125M comparisons. Moderate GC pressure over a long run.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep a separate running counter n_ok instead of scanning results.filter() in writeManifestIncremental. Only append the minimum fields to results that downstream consumers need (status, ticker). For the full manifest write at run end, stream the per-ticker details to _manifest-full.json incrementally rather than accumulating in memory.

findings.title: topAllMust uses Runner.METHODS.find (O(M)) instead of pre-built METHODS_BY_ID map
findings.description: The topAllMust function resolves the defaultSortMethod metadata using Runner.METHODS.find(m => m.id === sortMethodId) at line 260. The METHODS_BY_ID Map was introduced at line 28 specifically to fix this O(M) pattern elsewhere in the file (lines 370, 469), but topAllMust was not updated. topAllMust is called once per mode (4-5 modes) on every report generation, so this is at most ~5 O(M) scans against ~83 methods = ~415 comparisons.
findings.mechanism: Runner.METHODS.find(m => m.id === sortMethodId) at line 260 scans up to ~83 methods per call. METHODS_BY_ID.get(sortMethodId) at O(1) is available and used at lines 370 and 469 in the same file.
findings.reproducer: Call generate-modes-report.js main(). The find() at line 260 runs once per mode tab.
findings.impact: Minimal on its own (~415 comparisons). Included because (a) it is an incomplete fix of an already-identified bug class, (b) if Runner.METHODS grows significantly the cost grows with it.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 260 with: const sortMethodMeta = sortMethodId ? METHODS_BY_ID.get(sortMethodId) : null;

findings.title: Redundant IC/CE arithmetic and metric-push duplication in gatherBySubProfile
findings.description: In gatherBySubProfile, the invested-capital (ic) and capital-employed (ce) values are computed twice per stock: once for globalBuckets (lines 103-113) and again for regionalBuckets (lines 119-129). The expressions are identical: ic = ta - (cash || 0) and ce = ta - (cash || 0). The entire three-metric push block (roic, roce, fcf-yield) is also duplicated verbatim for global and regional, meaning 6 conditional push operations per stock instead of 3.
findings.mechanism: Lines 103-106: ic = ta - (cash||0); globalBuckets[sp.id].roic.push(ni/ic). Lines 119-122: ic = ta - (cash||0); regionalBuckets[region][sp.id].roic.push(ni/ic). Identical computation, no side effects between them.
findings.reproducer: Call gatherBySubProfile with any stocks array of length N. IC and CE are each computed 2N times instead of N times.
findings.impact: Negligible at current scale (15k stocks, microsecond arithmetic). Maintenance hazard: if the IC formula ever changes, only one copy may be updated, creating a silent divergence between global and regional bucket calculations.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute ic and ce once before the push blocks. Extract a helper: function _pushMetrics(bucket, ni, oi, ta, cash, fcf, mc) { if (ni != null && ta != null) { const ic = ta-(cash||0); if(ic>0) bucket.roic.push(ni/ic); } ... } Call for both globalBuckets[sp.id] and regionalBuckets[region][sp.id].

findings.title: sector-medians-compute CLI does not exclude underscore-prefixed internal snapshot files
findings.description: The CLI entry point filters with f !== '_manifest.json' but does not exclude other underscore-prefixed internal files such as _manifest-full.json. generate-modes-report.js and snapshot-methods-history.js both use !f.startsWith('_') as the canonical exclusion rule. If _manifest-full.json is present in the snapshots directory, it passes the filter and is fed to JSON.parse then computeMedians, where it is silently skipped by classify (no valid sub-profile).
findings.mechanism: fs.readdirSync(snapshotDir).filter(f => f.endsWith('.json') && f !== '_manifest.json'): the string equality check only excludes the exact filename '_manifest.json'. Any other internal file starting with '_' passes.
findings.reproducer: Run `node methods/sector-medians-compute.js ./snapshots` in a directory that contains _manifest-full.json.
findings.impact: Silent: the non-stock file is skipped after a wasted JSON.parse of the ~1-2 MB _manifest-full.json. No data corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 350 filter to: .filter(f => f.endsWith('.json') && !f.startsWith('_')) to match the canonical exclusion rule.

findings.title: snapshot-methods-history healthDir uses relative path, breaks when CWD != __dirname
findings.description: The pipeline-health directory is created as const healthDir = './pipeline-health' (relative path, resolves against process.cwd()). generate-modes-report.js uses path.join(__dirname, 'pipeline-health') with an explicit comment 'use __dirname-relative path so the script works from any CWD' (F-ME-024). If snapshot-methods-history.js is invoked from a different working directory, the health file is written to the wrong location.
findings.mechanism: healthDir = './pipeline-health' resolves relative to process.cwd() which is not guaranteed to equal __dirname. generate-modes-report.js solved this explicitly.
findings.reproducer: Run `node /absolute/path/to/snapshot-methods-history.js` from a directory that is not the screener-data root.
findings.impact: Pipeline health check reads the wrong (or missing) health file, causing downstream CI gates to fail or silently pass incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 169 to: const healthDir = path.join(__dirname, 'pipeline-health'); -- matches the fix already applied in generate-modes-report.js.
